---
applyTo: "web/**/*.{templ,css,js}"
---

# Web Frontend Review

Stillwater's frontend is templ + HTMX + Tailwind v4 + a small set of first-party
ES5 JavaScript modules. The conventions below codify patterns that already
exist in the codebase; new frontend work should match them rather than invent
parallel approaches.

## HTMX swap conventions

The codebase uses four `hx-swap` values and they are not interchangeable:

- `outerHTML` -- the response replaces the target element including its tag.
  Use this when the server owns the element's identity (id, classes, attrs).
- `innerHTML` -- the response replaces children only. Use when the wrapping
  element is part of the page layout that should survive the swap.
- `none` -- no DOM swap; the request is fired for its side effect
  (`HX-Trigger` header, status code change, server-side state).
- `beforeend` -- only justified for append-only streams; if you find yourself
  reaching for it, check whether a server-rendered list re-render fits better.

Out-of-band swaps (`hx-swap-oob` on a child of the response, or the matching
attribute on a top-level fragment) are silent if the target id is missing from
the live DOM. There are 22 OOB sites in the codebase; when adding another,
verify the target id exists in the page that triggers the request -- the
failure mode is "nothing happens" with no console error.

## `HX-Trigger` response header

Server-emitted events use the `namespace:event-name` form
(`dashboard:action-resolved`, `artist:show-violations-tab`,
`oobeConflictRefresh`, `clobberRecheck`). Listeners on the client side use
`hx-trigger="<event> from:body"`.

The header MUST NOT be set on a failure path. `handlers_fix_test.go` already
asserts this for the fix endpoint; the rule generalises: if the operation
failed, do not trigger a queue refresh, a toast dismissal, or any other
listener that would erase the user's view of the failure. Set the header only
on the success path, or unconditionally clear it before returning on error.

## `HX-Request` branching for error responses

Handlers that serve both HTMX requests and JSON API callers branch on
`r.Header.Get("HX-Request") == "true"`. The HTMX branch returns the
`components.ErrorToast(level, message)` server-rendered fragment so HTMX can
swap it into a toast container; the JSON branch returns the standard error
envelope. Keep the messages parallel: do not let the HTMX message say "Sync
skipped" while the JSON body says "internal error". See
[go-api](go-api.instructions.md) for warning-string discipline that applies on
both branches.

## CSRF token threading

State-changing fetches from `.templ` inline `<script>` or first-party JS read
the `csrf_token` cookie and send it as the `X-CSRF-Token` request header. The
canonical implementation lives in `web/static/js/preferences.js`; copy from
there rather than reinventing the cookie-parse regex. Multiple subtly
different inline regexes are not an accepted pattern -- the duplication makes
future tightening (SameSite, prefix change) require a sweep across every
inline copy.

## Templ component reuse

High-reuse components already exist: `Badge`, `Icon`, `Toast`, `ErrorToast`,
`ConfirmModal`, `BottomSheet`, `ContextMenu`. Before adding a new component,
grep `web/components/` for an equivalent -- in most cases the reusable form
already exists and the new code should call it. Components that grow case
parameters (`variant`, `kind`, `mode`) are generally preferable to two
near-duplicate components.

## Templ generated-file discipline

Every `*.templ` file has a paired `*_templ.go` generated by `templ generate`
and committed to the repo. `make generate` regenerates both templ and
Tailwind; `scripts/check-generated.sh` (called by the pre-push gate) verifies
that any `.templ` change is accompanied by a regenerated `_templ.go`. Never
hand-edit `_templ.go`; the next regeneration will clobber the edit and the
review surface lies about what shipped.

## i18n

`internal/i18n` exposes exactly one runtime translator entry point:
`i18n.TFromCtx(ctx)`. New user-visible strings call it (or `Tn` for plurals)
and add the key to `internal/i18n/locales/en.json`. The translator is set on
the request context by middleware, so templ components receive it for free
via `ctx`. Do not hardcode user-visible English in templ files -- the
i18n discipline is what makes locale overrides work without a code change.

## First-party JavaScript modules

The files under `web/static/js/` that are not vendored (`htmx.min.js`,
`chart.min.js`, `cropper.min.js`, `Sortable.min.js`) follow a uniform pattern:

- IIFE wrapper: `(function () { 'use strict'; ... })()`.
- ES5 only. No `const`/`let`, no arrow functions, no `Set`, no
  `NodeList.forEach`. The constraint is explicit in `sse.js` and is the floor
  for every first-party module; running in older WebViews (and the
  no-build-step posture) is the reason.
- A header comment that names the module's purpose and lists its Public API.
- A single `window.sw<Name>` global is the only export surface
  (`window.swSidebar`, `window.swPreferences`, `window.swSSE`,
  `window.swFilterFlyout`). Avoid leaking other names onto `window`.
- Base-path aware via `meta[name="htmx-base-path"]`; never hardcode
  `/api/v1/...` in a fetch URL or sub-path deployments break.

When adding a new module, copy the structure from `preferences.js` or
`sidebar.js` rather than starting from a blank file.

## CSS layering

Tailwind v4 with the CSS-first config: `web/static/css/input.css` is the
source, `web/static/css/styles.css` is the generated output (do not edit).
`design-tokens.css` carries CSS custom properties consumed by both Tailwind
utilities and the `sw-` class layer. Dark mode is gated by the `.dark`
ancestor class via `@custom-variant dark` -- it is not Tailwind's default
`prefers-color-scheme` setup.

Project-specific compound treatments use the `sw-` class prefix
(`sw-card`, `sw-glass-bg`, `sw-conflict-state`, ...). Reach for Tailwind
utilities first; lift to a `sw-` class when the same combination repeats more
than twice or when it encapsulates a design token (frosted glass, acrylic
card). Do not stack two Tailwind display utilities on the same element
(`hidden inline-flex`); the cascade keeps the later one and the earlier is
dead weight.

## Accessibility floor

The existing components carry a substantial a11y baseline: `role="switch"`
plus `aria-checked` for iOS-style toggles, `role="status"` with `aria-live`
for non-modal updates, `role="alert"` for error regions, `aria-modal` plus
`aria-labelledby` for dialogs, `aria-controls` and `aria-expanded` for
disclosure widgets. New interactive components must keep this floor. When
adding a form element, run a manual screen-reader / keyboard pass before the
first push -- the deterministic gate does not catch missing roles or
unlabeled controls.
